title: Practice Set 10 — Operational Troubleshooting
description: Diagnose and resolve production incidents: differentiate driver vs executor OOM errors, identify straggler tasks from data skew, detect silent data loss after joins, classify failures from error logs, and resolve a multi-symptom production incident using the complete diagnostic toolkit from the mid-level track.

datasets.description: NordGrid Silver readings. 500 rows. 20 rows have empty substation_id (will be silently dropped by inner join). Used for silent data loss detection task.

tasks.title: Diagnose an OOM on the Driver

description.context: NordGrid's Gold billing pipeline crashed with a java.lang.OutOfMemoryError on the driver. The pipeline processed 1.2M Silver readings successfully through filtering, joining, and aggregation. The crash happened at the very end, when the pipeline called .collect() to bring the 12-row Gold summary to the driver for logging before writing.
description.task: Read the error log and pipeline code below. Diagnose why the OOM occurred on the driver, identify the dangerous operation, and recommend the fix.
description.validationHint: collect() brings ALL rows to the driver's memory. If the DataFrame was accidentally unfiltered, collect() on 1.2M rows exhausts the 4 GB driver.

errorLog.label: Error log (truncated)
errorLog.content:
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space
	at java.base/java.util.Arrays.copyOf(Arrays.java:3512)
	at org.apache.spark.sql.execution.SparkPlan.executeCollect(SparkPlan.scala:422)
	at org.apache.spark.sql.Dataset.collectFromPlan(Dataset.scala:4144)
	at org.apache.spark.sql.Dataset.collect(Dataset.scala:3624)
	at org.apache.spark.sql.Dataset.$$anonfun$collect$1(Dataset.scala:3624)

Driver memory: 4 GB
Executor memory: 16 GB (4 executors)

codeBlock.label: Pipeline code — the bug is near the end
codeBlock.content:
df_silver = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')  # 1.2M rows
df_subs = spark.read.parquet('reference/substations/')

# Enrichment join (correct)
df_enriched = df_silver.join(broadcast(df_subs), on='substation_id')

# Gold aggregation (correct)
df_gold = df_enriched.groupBy('region').agg(
    spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'),
    count('*').alias('reading_count')
)

# BUG: developer meant to collect df_gold (12 rows) for logging
# but accidentally collected df_enriched (1.2M rows)
result = df_enriched.collect()  # <-- OOM: 1.2M rows to 4 GB driver
print(f'Gold summary: {result}')

df_gold.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/regional/')

fields.label: What operation caused the driver OOM?
options: The broadcast join consumed too much driver memory
The groupBy aggregation exceeded the driver's memory budget
.collect() on df_enriched (1.2M rows) transferred the entire enriched dataset to the 4 GB driver, exhausting its heap
The Parquet write failed due to insufficient driver memory
fields.correctAnswer: .collect() on df_enriched (1.2M rows) transferred the entire enriched dataset to the 4 GB driver, exhausting its heap

fields.label: What was the developer's likely intention, and what is the fix?
options: Remove collect() entirely — it's never needed in pipelines
The developer meant to collect df_gold (12 rows) for logging, not df_enriched (1.2M rows). Fix: change df_enriched.collect() to df_gold.collect(). 12 rows fits easily in 4 GB.
Increase driver memory to 64 GB to handle the collect()
Replace collect() with .take(10) to limit the rows
fields.correctAnswer: The developer meant to collect df_gold (12 rows) for logging, not df_enriched (1.2M rows). Fix: change df_enriched.collect() to df_gold.collect(). 12 rows fits easily in 4 GB.

fields.label: How can you distinguish a driver OOM from an executor OOM in error logs?
options: There is no way to distinguish them
Driver OOM: the stack trace shows 'main' thread and SparkPlan.executeCollect. Executor OOM: the error shows 'Container killed by YARN' or 'ExecutorLostFailure' with 'exit code 137'.
Driver OOM always mentions 'Java heap space'; executor OOM mentions 'Python memory'
Driver OOM crashes the entire application; executor OOM only affects one task
fields.correctAnswer: Driver OOM: the stack trace shows 'main' thread and SparkPlan.executeCollect. Executor OOM: the error shows 'Container killed by YARN' or 'ExecutorLostFailure' with 'exit code 137'.

tasks.title: Diagnose an OOM on an Executor

description.context: NordGrid's Silver aggregation pipeline crashed with an executor OOM. The pipeline groups 1.2M readings by meter_id and month (14.4M output groups). The cluster has 4 executors with 8 GB each. The error log shows YARN killed the executor container.
description.task: Read the error log and stage metrics. Diagnose why the executor ran out of memory, identify which operation caused it, and recommend fixes.
description.validationHint: Executor OOM during aggregation means the hash table for the groupBy exceeded the executor's memory budget. Compare the aggregation's memory requirements to the available executor memory.

errorLog.label: YARN error log
errorLog.content:
WARN YarnSchedulerBackend$YarnSchedulerEndpoint: Container marked as failed:
container_1711234567890_0001_01_000003 on host: worker-node-2.
Exit status: 137

ExecutorLostFailure (executor 2 exited caused by one of the running tasks)
Reason: Container killed by YARN for exceeding memory limits.
8.2 GB of 8.0 GB physical memory used.

Failed task: Stage 1, Task 88 (attempt 3 of 4)
Task was running HashAggregate with keys=[meter_id, month]

stageMetrics.label: Stage 1 metrics (before crash)
stageMetrics.content:
Stage 1: Exchange + HashAggregate (meter_id, month)
Shuffle partitions: 16
Shuffle read: 4.8 GB (300 MB per task)
Spill (Memory): 14 GB across all tasks
Spill (Disk): 6.2 GB
Failed tasks: 4 (all on executor 2, all OOM)

Per-task memory: ~1.5 GB (6 GB usable / 4 cores)
Hash table for 900K groups per partition: ~1.8 GB (exceeds 1.5 GB budget)

fields.label: What operation caused the executor OOM?
options: The file scan read too much data into executor memory

The HashAggregate's hash table for groupBy(meter_id, month) exceeded the per-task memory budget. With 16 shuffle partitions, each task processes 300 MB input that expands to a 1.8 GB hash table — exceeding the 1.5 GB per-task budget.
The shuffle read transferred too much data to the executor
A Python UDF consumed all executor memory
fields.correctAnswer: The HashAggregate's hash table for groupBy(meter_id, month) exceeded the per-task memory budget. With 16 shuffle partitions, each task processes 300 MB input that expands to a 1.8 GB hash table — exceeding the 1.5 GB per-task budget.

fields.label: Why does YARN show 'exit status: 137'?
options: 137 is a generic Spark error code
Exit code 137 = 128 + 9 (SIGKILL). YARN killed the container with SIGKILL because it exceeded its memory limit. This is the standard OOM kill signal on Linux/YARN.
137 indicates a network timeout
137 is the number of the failed task
fields.correctAnswer: Exit code 137 = 128 + 9 (SIGKILL). YARN killed the container with SIGKILL because it exceeded its memory limit. This is the standard OOM kill signal on Linux/YARN.

fields.label: What are two complementary fixes?
options: Increase driver memory + reduce executor count
Increase shuffle partitions (16 → 48+) to reduce per-task data AND increase executor memory (8 GB → 16 GB) to expand the per-task budget. Both reduce the ratio of data-to-memory.
Reduce shuffle partitions + decrease executor memory
Switch from groupBy to collect + Python-side aggregation
fields.correctAnswer: Increase shuffle partitions (16 → 48+) to reduce per-task data AND increase executor memory (8 GB → 16 GB) to expand the per-task budget. Both reduce the ratio of data-to-memory.

fields.label: The task failed 3 times before the executor was killed. What does 'attempt 3 of 4' indicate?
options: Spark retried the task 3 times on the same executor before YARN killed the container. The retries are pointless because the OOM is deterministic — the same data causes the same OOM every time on the same executor.
The task succeeded on the 3rd attempt
Spark tried 3 different executors before giving up
YARN allows 4 total attempts across the cluster
fields.correctAnswer: Spark retried the task 3 times on the same executor before YARN killed the container. The retries are pointless because the OOM is deterministic — the same data causes the same OOM every time on the same executor.

tasks.title: Identify the Straggler and Its Root Cause

description.context: NordGrid's Gold regional pipeline completed but took 18 minutes instead of the usual 3 minutes. No errors were raised. The Spark UI shows that Stage 2 has one task that took 17 minutes while the other 199 tasks finished in under 5 seconds.
description.task: Examine the task metrics and data distribution. Identify the straggler, determine the root cause, and choose the appropriate mitigation from the mid-level toolkit.
description.validationHint: Cross-reference the straggler task's data volume with the key distribution to identify the skewed key.

taskMetrics.label: Stage 2 task summary
taskMetrics.content:
Total tasks: 200 | Stage duration: 17.2 min
Median task: 2.8s | P95: 4.1s | Max: 17.1 min (Task 42)

Task 42 details:
  Records:      672,000
  Shuffle read:  688 MB
  Spill (Disk):  3.4 GB
  GC time:       62s
  Executor:      executor-3

keyDistribution.label: Top 5 substation_ids by row count in Silver readings

fields.label: Which key value is causing the straggler?
What is the skew ratio (max task records / median task records)?
fields.rationale: Median task ~2,800 records (from 2.8s duration). Max task 672,000 records. Ratio: 672000/2800 = 240x. By data volume: 688 MB / ~2.9 MB median = 237x.

fields.label: What is the best mitigation from the mid-level toolkit?
options: Increase shuffle partitions from 200 to 2000

If this is a join: use broadcast() on the small table to eliminate the shuffle entirely. If this is a groupBy: increase shuffle partitions + enable AQE. The approach depends on which operation Stage 2 performs.
Add more executors to process the straggler faster
Filter out SUB-001 and process it separately
fields.correctAnswer: If this is a join: use broadcast() on the small table to eliminate the shuffle entirely. If this is a groupBy: increase shuffle partitions + enable AQE. The approach depends on which operation Stage 2 performs.
fields.distractorRationale: Option 1 doesn't split a single key's data across partitions (hash partitioning puts all SUB-001 rows in one partition regardless of count). Option 3 doesn't help — one task runs on one core. Option 4 works but is complex. The correct answer acknowledges that the fix depends on the operation type.

tasks.title: Detect and Fix Silent Data Loss After a Join

description.context: NordGrid's enrichment pipeline inner-joins 500 Silver readings with the 40-row substations table on substation_id. The pipeline completes successfully with no errors. But the output has 480 rows instead of 500. Twenty readings were silently dropped because they have empty substation_id values that don't match any substation. The billing team doesn't notice until month-end reconciliation.
description.task: Diagnose the silent data loss, then write a pipeline that detects it automatically. Add a pre-join assertion that checks for empty/null join keys, and a post-join assertion that verifies the output count matches the input count (for left joins) or is within an expected range (for inner joins). Store the fixed output in a variable called enriched.
description.validationHint: The 20 rows with empty substation_id are silently dropped by the inner join. A left join would preserve them with null enrichment columns. The pre-join check should flag the empty keys before the join runs.

silentLoss.label: Pipeline output vs expected
silentLoss.content:
Input readings:  500 rows
Output enriched: 480 rows  ← 20 rows silently lost
No errors raised. Pipeline status: SUCCESS.

Missing rows: 20 readings with empty substation_id
Impact: 20 meters not billed for March → ~$48,000 revenue loss

codeBlock.label: Current pipeline (inner join, no validation)
codeBlock.content:
df_readings = spark.read.csv('PM10_data_readings_silver.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
df_subs = spark.read.csv('PM10_data_substations.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# Inner join silently drops rows with empty substation_id
enriched = df_readings.join(df_subs, on='substation_id', how='inner')
# 480 rows instead of 500. No error.

fields.label: Why did 20 rows disappear without an error?
options: The inner join has a bug that randomly drops rows

The 20 readings have empty substation_id. An inner join requires a match in BOTH tables. Empty strings don't match any substation_id in the reference table, so those rows are silently excluded. This is correct inner join behavior — the problem is that no one validated the output.
The substations table is missing 20 substations
The readings file has corrupt rows that Spark skipped
fields.correctAnswer: The 20 readings have empty substation_id. An inner join requires a match in BOTH tables. Empty strings don't match any substation_id in the reference table, so those rows are silently excluded. This is correct inner join behavior — the problem is that no one validated the output.

fields.label: What join type would preserve all readings while still enriching matched ones?
options: Cross join — matches every reading with every substation
Left join — keeps all 500 readings, fills substation columns with null for unmatched rows
Anti join — returns only unmatched readings
Semi join — returns matched readings without substation columns
fields.correctAnswer: Left join — keeps all 500 readings, fills substation columns with null for unmatched rows

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, count as spark_count

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-silent-loss-fix') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

df_readings = spark.read.csv('PM10_data_readings_silver.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
df_subs = spark.read.csv('PM10_data_substations.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

input_count = df_readings.count()
print(f"Input readings: {input_count}")

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# 1. Pre-join check: count rows with null/empty substation_id. Log a warning.
# 2. Use LEFT join instead of inner to preserve all readings
# 3. Post-join assertion: output count must equal input count (for left join)
# 4. Store in 'enriched'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"Enriched rows: {enriched.count()}")
print(f"Null substation_name (unmatched): {enriched.filter(col('substation_name').isNull()).count()}")
assert enriched.count() == input_count, f"Data loss! {enriched.count()} != {input_count}"
print("No data loss detected.")

assertions.description: 20 rows have null substation_name (unmatched but preserved)

tasks.title: Classify Failure Types from Error Logs

description.context: NordGrid's on-call engineer receives three error alerts from different pipelines overnight. Each error has a different root cause: one is a driver failure, one is an executor failure, and one is a data corruption issue. The engineer must triage them by classifying each error and determining the fix priority.
description.task: Read the three error logs below. For each, classify the failure type, identify the responsible component, and assign fix priority.
description.validationHint: Driver errors crash the entire application. Executor errors can be retried. Data corruption is silent and highest priority because it produces wrong results without any error.

errorA.label: Error A — Gold billing pipeline
errorA.content:
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space
  at org.apache.spark.sql.execution.SparkPlan.executeCollect(SparkPlan.scala:422)
  at org.apache.spark.sql.Dataset.collect(Dataset.scala:3624)

Pipeline status: FAILED
Driver memory: 4 GB

errorB.label: Error B — Silver aggregation pipeline
errorB.content:
WARN YarnSchedulerBackend: Container marked as failed:
  container_0001_01_000004 on host: worker-3
  Exit status: 137
ExecutorLostFailure (executor 3 exited caused by running tasks)
Reason: Container killed by YARN for exceeding memory limits.
12.1 GB of 12.0 GB physical memory used.

Stage 2, Task 147 failed after 4 attempts.
Pipeline status: FAILED

errorC.label: Error C — Silver readings pipeline
errorC.content:
Pipeline status: SUCCESS
Rows written: 1,200,000

BUT: downstream Gold pipeline reports SUM(daily_kw) for NORTH_COAST
is 15% lower than yesterday. No schema change detected.
Investigation reveals: 180,000 readings have daily_kw = 0.0
instead of actual values. Source system sent zeros during a
maintenance window. Pipeline treated zeros as valid data.

fields.label: Error A classification:
options: Executor OOM — increase executor memory
Driver OOM — collect() on a large DataFrame exhausted the 4 GB driver heap. Fix: collect the aggregated result instead, or increase driver memory.
Data corruption — wrong values written
Network failure — lost connection to cluster
fields.correctAnswer: Driver OOM — collect() on a large DataFrame exhausted the 4 GB driver heap. Fix: collect the aggregated result instead, or increase driver memory.

fields.label: Error B classification:
options: Driver OOM — the main thread ran out of memory
Executor OOM — YARN killed executor 3 (exit 137 = SIGKILL) for exceeding 12 GB container limit. Fix: increase executor memory or shuffle partitions.
Task timeout — task 147 ran too long
Data skew — but no memory issue
fields.correctAnswer: Executor OOM — YARN killed executor 3 (exit 137 = SIGKILL) for exceeding 12 GB container limit. Fix: increase executor memory or shuffle partitions.

fields.label: Error C classification:
options: Not an error — the pipeline succeeded

Silent data corruption — the pipeline wrote 180K zero-value readings as valid data. No error was raised because zeros are technically valid doubles. The damage is only visible in downstream aggregations.
Schema mismatch — the daily_kw column type changed
Executor failure — some executors dropped data
fields.correctAnswer: Silent data corruption — the pipeline wrote 180K zero-value readings as valid data. No error was raised because zeros are technically valid doubles. The damage is only visible in downstream aggregations.

fields.label: Which error should be fixed FIRST (highest priority)?
options: Error A — driver OOM crashes the entire application
Error B — executor OOM prevents the pipeline from completing

Error C — silent data corruption is highest priority because the pipeline 'succeeded' but wrote wrong data. Errors A and B are visible failures that block the pipeline. Error C is invisible and actively corrupting downstream results.
All three have equal priority
fields.correctAnswer: Error C — silent data corruption is highest priority because the pipeline 'succeeded' but wrote wrong data. Errors A and B are visible failures that block the pipeline. Error C is invisible and actively corrupting downstream results.
fields.distractorRationale: Errors A and B are loud failures — the pipeline crashes, ops is alerted, no bad data is written. Error C is a silent failure — bad data is actively flowing to dashboards and billing systems. Silent failures are always higher priority than loud failures because they cause damage while appearing healthy.

description.context: NordGrid's on-call engineer is paged at 07:15. The Gold billing pipeline failed at 06:45. The Silver pipeline completed at 06:30 but downstream reports show 4% lower revenue than expected. The engineer must diagnose both issues: why the Gold pipeline crashed AND why Silver's data is suspicious. The evidence spans error logs, stage metrics, and data samples. This is the capstone incident — it requires every diagnostic skill from the mid-level track.
description.task: Examine all evidence below. Build the incident report: identify both problems, determine their root causes, prioritize the fixes, and implement the higher-priority fix. Store the fixed enrichment result in a variable called fixed.
description.validationHint: Two problems: (1) the Gold pipeline crashed due to an executor OOM from data skew, (2) the Silver pipeline silently lost 20 readings with empty substation_id during an inner join. Fix priority: the silent data loss first (it's corrupting data NOW), then the OOM (it's blocking a pipeline but not corrupting).

goldError.label: Gold pipeline error log
goldError.content:
WARN YarnSchedulerBackend: Container marked as failed:
  Exit status: 137
ExecutorLostFailure: Container killed by YARN, 16.2 GB of 16.0 GB used.
Stage 2 (HashAggregate by region), Task 42 failed.

Stage 2 task metrics:
  Median task: 3s | Max task (42): crashed at 8 min
  Task 42 shuffle read: 688 MB (vs median 5 MB)
  Cause: SUB-001 has 56% of readings → skewed partition

silverSuspicion.label: Silver pipeline anomaly
silverSuspicion.content:
Silver pipeline status: SUCCESS (no errors)
Silver row count: 480 (expected: 500)

Investigation: the Silver enrichment uses INNER JOIN on substation_id.
20 readings have empty substation_id → silently dropped.
Impact: 20 meters not billed → ~$48K monthly revenue loss.

dataSample.label: Dropped readings sample
dataSample.content:
meter_id  | daily_kw | date       | region      | substation_id
M-00481   | 2847.3   | 2026-03-12 | NORTH_COAST | (empty)
M-00489   | 1523.8   | 2026-03-14 | EAST        | (empty)
M-00495   | 3901.2   | 2026-03-11 | SOUTH       | (empty)

fields.label: Problem 1 (Gold crash): What is the root cause?
options: Insufficient driver memory
Data skew on SUB-001 (56% of readings) causes a 688 MB partition in the groupBy, exceeding executor memory during HashAggregate
Too many shuffle partitions
Corrupt Parquet files in Silver
fields.correctAnswer: Data skew on SUB-001 (56% of readings) causes a 688 MB partition in the groupBy, exceeding executor memory during HashAggregate

fields.label: Problem 2 (Silver data loss): What is the root cause?
options: The inner join dropped 20 readings with empty substation_id — no error raised, no validation caught it
The source system sent fewer rows than expected
A filter removed the 20 rows
The Parquet write truncated the file
fields.correctAnswer: The inner join dropped 20 readings with empty substation_id — no error raised, no validation caught it

fields.label: Which problem should be fixed FIRST?
options: Problem 1 (Gold crash) — the pipeline is down and needs to be restored

Problem 2 (Silver data loss) — silent data loss is actively corrupting billing. The Gold crash is visible and blocking, but no bad data is flowing. The Silver loss is invisible and producing wrong bills NOW.
Both simultaneously — assign one engineer to each
Neither — wait for the next scheduled run to see if they self-resolve
fields.correctAnswer: Problem 2 (Silver data loss) — silent data loss is actively corrupting billing. The Gold crash is visible and blocking, but no bad data is flowing. The Silver loss is invisible and producing wrong bills NOW.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, broadcast

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-incident-resolution') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

df_readings = spark.read.csv('PM10_data_readings_silver.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
df_subs = spark.read.csv('PM10_data_substations.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

input_count = df_readings.count()

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Fix Problem 2 (highest priority): Silver data loss
# 1. Pre-join: log count of empty substation_id rows
# 2. Use LEFT join to preserve all readings
# 3. Post-join: assert output count == input count
# Store in 'fixed'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"Input: {input_count}")
print(f"Fixed output: {fixed.count()}")
assert fixed.count() == input_count, f"Data loss! {fixed.count()} != {input_count}"
print("Silent data loss fixed.")
print(f"Unmatched rows preserved: {fixed.filter(col('substation_name').isNull()).count()}")

assertions.description: 20 rows have null substation_name (preserved, not dropped)

conceptsCombined: Executor OOM diagnosis — YARN exit 137, skewed partition (Task 2, PM7)
Data skew identification from task metrics (Task 3, PM2-3, PM7)
Silent data loss from inner join with null/empty keys (Task 4, PM3, PM6)
Incident prioritization — silent corruption > loud failure (Task 5)
Left join for data preservation (Junior M7, Task 4)
synthesisNotes.rationale: This is the capstone incident of the entire mid-level track. It presents two concurrent problems with different severities: a loud failure (OOM crash) and a silent failure (data loss). The student must diagnose both, prioritize correctly (silent > loud), and implement the higher-priority fix. Every diagnostic skill from PM1-PM9 contributes: plan reading, skew detection, join behavior, validation, and incident classification. The key mid-level insight: the scariest production problems are the ones that don't raise errors.

selfCheck.evidenceRealistic: Error logs use real Spark/YARN error formats (exit 137, executeCollect stack trace, Container killed). The silent data loss scenario mirrors a real production incident where inner joins silently drop rows with null join keys.
selfCheck.theoryPracticeBoundary: Theory teaches failure types individually. Practice requires diagnosing compound incidents with multiple concurrent failures, prioritizing based on data impact (not error severity), and applying the complete mid-level toolkit to resolve them.
